normative_source.standard_name: OWASP Mobile Application Security Verification Standard
normative_source.source_section: MASVS-CODE: Code Quality and Build Settings — MASVS-CODE-1 (page 28)
normative_source.requirement_title: The app requires an up-to-date platform version.
normative_source.normative_excerpt: Per OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 §MASVS-CODE-1 (Description): Every release of the mobile OS includes security patches and new security features. By supporting older versions, apps stay vulnerable to well-known threats. This control ensures that the app is running on an up-to-date platform version so that users have the latest security protections.
normative_source.normative_summary: Mobile apps must declare and enforce a minimum platform OS version (iOS deployment target, Android minSdkVersion) that is at or above the version required for all declared platform-secure mechanisms — secure storage APIs, biometric authentication frameworks, network security defaults, sandbox features, and runtime protections. Supporting older OS versions exposes the app to known platform-level vulnerabilities patched in newer releases; the minimum-version floor is a build-time gate against this exposure.

source_rights.license_notes: Source license recorded from the downloaded OWASP MASVS v2.1.0 PDF artifact. Live website may differ; this corpus pins the artifact license. Verbatim excerpts include attribution per CC-BY-SA-4.0 §3.

applicability.human_note: Applies at Tier 1+ as a conservative corpus-level gate. Per Constitution Pillar 3 (Cryptography & Key Material), platform version floor discipline is universal — the dedicated trace SEC-PILLAR-3-PLATFORM-VERSION-FLOOR (new in Constitution v0.5) is a Tier 1-5 control because every app declares a minimum OS version regardless of tier. The Constitution v0.5 trace definition references a `security_manifest.minimum_platform_version` declaration that is not yet a dedicated field in the blueprint schema; current applicability uses `security_manifest.app_tier >= 1` as proxy, and evidence flows through build-setting surfaces (iOS IPHONEOS_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET, Android minSdkVersion). A future schema enhancement (banked) will add the dedicated minimum_platform_version field. In practice every Intrinsic Code app at Tier 1+ requires a declared minimum-version floor because secure storage APIs, biometric frameworks, and network defaults all carry per-OS-version availability matrices that the floor enforces against.

intrinsic_interpretation.mapping_rationale: Constitution Pillar 3's SEC-PILLAR-3-PLATFORM-VERSION-FLOOR trace was added in v0.5 specifically to provide a 1:1 mapping to MASVS-CODE-1. The trace requires the manifest to declare a minimum platform OS version, with the floor enforced to be at or above the version required for all declared platform-secure primitives, secure storage APIs, and runtime protections — and builds targeting a platform version below the declared floor are rejected at blueprint validation. This directly implements MASVS-CODE-1's requirement that the app require an up-to-date platform version. The mapping is direct because the trace and the MASVS control state the same requirement. Implementation correctness for the chosen floor (auditing the declared minimum against currently-supported OS levels at build time, validating against the platform-primitives the manifest declares, recurring re-evaluation on platform vendor end-of-life announcements) requires auditor judgment beyond manifest declaration. The mapping is direct at the control-statement level, but the auditor evaluation chain (file_standards_auditor + reaper-mobile + reaper-dependencies) is necessary for full evaluation of correctness.

expected_evidence_patterns: iOS: Xcode project declares IPHONEOS_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET at a value matching or exceeding the declared minimum platform version in the blueprint
iOS: Info.plist's MinimumOSVersion (set by Xcode from deployment target) matches the declared floor; build fails if mismatched
Android: app's build.gradle declares minSdkVersion (or minSdk) at a value matching or exceeding the declared minimum platform version in the blueprint
Android: AndroidManifest.xml's <uses-sdk android:minSdkVersion="N"> matches the declared floor; build fails if mismatched

Build configurations on both platforms align with the blueprint's declared minimum (currently captured via PIPELINE_LANGUAGE tier-floor conventions and blueprint-validation; future schema field security_manifest.minimum_platform_version will formalize the per-platform declaration)

Floor declared at or above the version required for the most-restrictive platform-secure mechanism the app uses (e.g., if app uses CryptoKit which requires iOS 13+, floor must be >= 13; if app uses BiometricPrompt strong-class which requires API 28+, floor must be >= 28); auditor cross-references the blueprint's declared minimum against the security_manifest's declared primitives, not against a non-existent minimum_platform_version field
Floor re-evaluation logged on platform vendor end-of-life announcements (Apple security update support windows, Google Android version support cycles)
failure_indicators: iOS: IPHONEOS_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET set below currently-supported iOS versions (Apple supports the previous two major versions for security updates; targeting iOS versions outside this window exposes the app to unpatched OS-level vulnerabilities)

Android: minSdkVersion set to a value below Google's current security-patch support window (typically the most recent ~3 major API levels); targeting older API levels exposes the app to known platform vulnerabilities

Floor mismatched against declared platform-secure primitives — e.g., security_manifest declares use of CryptoKit but the blueprint's effective minimum platform version is iOS 12 (CryptoKit requires iOS 13)

No declared minimum platform version in the blueprint (whether via dedicated schema field where one exists or via constitution_traces[] referencing SEC-PILLAR-3-PLATFORM-VERSION-FLOOR with target version) — blueprint validation should reject this; if it doesn't, the LOAD-time gate is bypassed

Build pipeline allows targeting platform versions below the declared floor — the floor is documented but not enforced (declared-but-not-enforced is a violation per Constitution's blueprint-validation requirement)

Apps that ship to multiple platforms with one platform's floor below the secure-mechanism requirement on that platform (e.g., security_manifest declares biometric strong-class on Android but minSdk is below the API level supporting it)
false_positive_traps: Platform-version handling inside test fixtures or test-only code paths (paths containing /test/, /tests/, /__tests__/, /spec/ OR functions/methods marked with @Test, @Ignore, @Disabled annotations). Test environments may legitimately use older API simulators for compatibility coverage; these are not production violations.

Apps that intentionally target a wider audience (e.g., consumer apps where the threat model accepts the risk of supporting last-gen devices with documented compensating controls) are NOT violations if the threat model explicitly accepts this and the security_manifest documents the accepted-risk posture with end-of-life timeline.

Long-tail enterprise apps targeting specific corporate-managed device fleets with documented OS-version lock-in are NOT violations as long as security_manifest declares the fleet-specific platform-floor posture; the violation surface is undeclared older-platform support, not declared.

Beta / TestFlight / internal-distribution builds with broader target ranges for compatibility testing are NOT violations if production builds enforce the declared floor — security_manifest must declare the build-variant scoping.

Apps that intentionally support an older platform for one specific feature (e.g., legacy device sensor access required for compatibility) are NOT violations if security_manifest scopes the floor exception to the specific feature and applies the standard floor everywhere else.

allowed_claims: uses Constitution Pillar 3 platform-version-floor discipline (SEC-PILLAR-3-PLATFORM-VERSION-FLOOR) as primary evidence toward MASVS-CODE-1 coverage

declares minimum platform OS version via build-setting surfaces (iOS IPHONEOS_DEPLOYMENT_TARGET, Android minSdkVersion) and blueprint-validation enforcement per MASVS-CODE-1 platform-best-practice requirement; future schema enhancement (banked) adds a dedicated security_manifest.minimum_platform_version field
enforces minimum-version floor at blueprint validation — builds targeting platform versions below the declared floor are rejected before code generation
claim_boundaries.claim_boundary_notes: Standard-level baseline (from manifest.json.standard_claim_policy) already covers OWASP-wide forbidden claims. No requirement-specific additions needed for MASVS-CODE-1. MASVS-CODE-1 is mapped to one Constitution v0.5 trace in mapping file v1.2: SEC-PILLAR-3-PLATFORM-VERSION-FLOOR (primary, direct). The trace was added in v0.5 specifically to provide a 1:1 mapping to CODE-1, so the mapping is direct rather than partial. Claims about platform-version security depend on the floor being aligned with currently-supported OS levels — declaring a floor below the OS vendor's security-update support window does not satisfy CODE-1 even though a floor is technically declared. Out of scope: OS-vendor patch deployment to end-user devices (Apple/Google control this, not the app); apps cannot force end-users onto patched OS versions, only enforce a minimum-version-floor at install/launch via platform store mechanisms. Tier routing handled downstream by Reaper Conclave matrix; the listed Reaper auditors consume this requirement when invoked for the bundle's tier and surface.
